narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 28, lower-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the 2nd decile—meaning your early grades placed you in the lower end compared to your peers, where averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 63% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students like you. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile, as being in the lower ranks there strongly signaled challenges in sustaining strong academic performance, unlike many peers who climbed higher. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing because your 3.10 is close to the typical 3.21 average, showing solid college work. Your Year 1 decile also nudged things toward failure, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that echoed in later years. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your LSAT stands out as notably below the average of 36.7, though it wasn't the top driver here, and your full-time status aligns with the vast majority who succeed. These patterns suggest the model saw your grades trajectory as the biggest risk, even with strengths like your GPA. While this stings, especially given your Hispanic background where systemic barriers can play a role in academic rankings, it's not the end of the road. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your LSAT through targeted prep—many students improve dramatically with practice—and consider programs or experiences that demonstrate upward academic momentum, like postgraduate courses or internships. Retake key tests if possible, and highlight any personal stories of resilience in your essays to show how you'll defy these statistical odds. Schools value growth, and with targeted steps, you can shift this prediction in your favor next time.
